expanded_description: Line segments are measurable portions of lines with definite endpoints. Understanding how to measure, compare, and calculate segment lengths using the Segment Addition Postulate is essential for solving geometric problems involving distance, perimeter, and coordinate geometry.

core_concept.principle: A line segment is a finite portion of a line bounded by two endpoints. Every segment has a measurable length, and the Segment Addition Postulate allows us to calculate total length by adding the lengths of its parts when a point lies between the endpoints.
core_concept.explanation: Think of a segment as a straight stick with two ends. You can measure exactly how long that stick is. If you break the stick at any point along its length, the two pieces add up to the original length. This simple idea—the whole equals the sum of its parts—is the Segment Addition Postulate, and it is one of the most practical tools in all of geometry.

image.alt: A segment AB shown as a horizontal line with endpoints A and B. A point C lies between A and B. Below: The same segment split into two pieces AC and CB, with arrows showing AC + CB = AB. Caption: 'The whole equals the sum of its parts.'
image.caption: Segment Addition Postulate: If point C lies between A and B, then AC + CB = AB.

sections.introduction: A line segment is the most basic measurable object in geometry. Unlike lines and rays, segments have finite length, making them essential for any discussion of distance and size.

key_concept.definition: A line segment is the part of a line consisting of two distinct endpoints and all points between them. The length of a segment is the distance between its endpoints, expressed in units of measurement.
key_concept.context: Segments are the building blocks of polygons—every side of a triangle, rectangle, or any polygon is a line segment. Measuring segments is the first step toward calculating perimeter, area, and working with coordinate geometry.

process_flow.process: Identify the Segment and Its Endpoints
process_flow.description: Locate the two points that define the segment. The segment includes both endpoints and every point between them.
process_flow.mechanism: A segment is named by its two endpoints with a bar over the letters. Segment AB (written AB with a horizontal bar) has endpoints A and B. The order of letters does not matter: AB— is the same as BA—.
process_flow.result: Clear identification of the geometric object and its boundaries.
applications: Segment PQ has endpoints P and Q
Segment XY— is identical to Segment YX—
Every side of polygon ABCD is a segment

process_flow.description: The length of a segment is the distance between its endpoints, denoted by the endpoint letters without a bar.
process_flow.mechanism: AB (without a bar) means 'the length of segment AB.' This is a numerical value with units (inches, centimeters, etc.) or a variable expression in algebra problems. Length is always a positive number.
process_flow.result: A numerical or algebraic representation of the segment's size.
applications: If AB = 5 cm, the distance from A to B is 5 centimeters
If PQ = x + 3, the length is expressed in terms of x
Length is always positive: AB > 0 for distinct points

process_flow.description: Segments can be compared using their lengths. Congruent segments have equal lengths.
process_flow.mechanism: If AB = CD, then segment AB is congruent to segment CD, written AB— ≅ CD—. Congruence means 'same size and shape'—for segments, this simply means same length. The symbol ≅ means 'is congruent to.'
process_flow.result: Ability to identify equal-length segments and use congruence in reasoning.
applications: If AB = 7 and CD = 7, then AB— ≅ CD—
In an equilateral triangle, all three sides are congruent
Midpoint of a segment creates two congruent segments

classifications.value: Standard ways to write and refer to segments and their lengths.
characteristics: AB with a bar (AB—) refers to the segment itself—the geometric figure
AB without a bar refers to the length—a number
The congruence symbol ≅ compares segments; the equals sign = compares lengths
Segments are labeled with capital letters at endpoints
classifications.behavior: Precise notation distinguishes the geometric object (segment) from its measurement (length).

examples.explanation:
'Draw AB—' means draw the segment.
'AB = 8 cm' means the length is 8 centimeters.
'AB— ≅ CD—' means the segments are congruent (same length).

Small matching tick marks on segments in a diagram indicate congruence.
Single tick, double tick, triple tick distinguish different pairs of congruent segments.

classifications.value: A point is between two other points if it lies on the segment connecting them.
characteristics: Point B is between A and C if A, B, C are collinear and AB + BC = AC
'Between' implies collinearity—the three points must lie on the same line
Exactly one point can be between the other two when three points are collinear
classifications.behavior: Betweenness is the key condition for applying the Segment Addition Postulate.

examples.explanation:
Points A, B, C on a line with B between A and C.
Then AB + BC = AC, and B is the unique point between A and C.
Points D, E, F forming a triangle—no point is between the other two because they are not collinear. Betweenness requires all points on one line.

image.alt: Diagram of segment AB with length 8 cm. Left: The segment drawn with endpoints labeled A and B, bar AB shown. Right: Notation key—'AB— = segment', 'AB = 8 cm = length', 'AB— ≅ CD— = congruent segments'. Ticker marks shown on matching segments.
image.caption: Segment Notation: Distinguishing the segment (figure) from its length (number) and congruence (relationship).

sections.introduction: The Segment Addition Postulate is a fundamental rule that connects the lengths of adjacent segments on a line. It formalizes the intuitive idea that the whole equals the sum of its parts.

key_concept.definition: If point B lies between points A and C on a line, then AB + BC = AC. In words: the length of the whole segment equals the sum of the lengths of the two smaller segments.
key_concept.context: This postulate is the foundation for solving countless geometry problems—finding unknown lengths, locating midpoints, setting up algebraic equations, and proving more advanced theorems about triangles and polygons.

classifications.value: If B is between A and C, then AB + BC = AC.
characteristics: Requires B to be between A and C (collinear and lying on segment AC)
The sum of the two parts equals the whole
Works with numbers, variables, and algebraic expressions
Can be rearranged: AC - AB = BC or AC - BC = AB
classifications.behavior: The postulate lets you find any one of the three lengths if you know the other two. Add parts to get the whole; subtract a part from the whole to get the other part.

examples.explanation:
If AB = 5 cm and BC = 7 cm, with B between A and C:
AC = AB + BC = 5 + 7 = 12 cm

If AC = 20 m and AB = 8 m, with B between A and C:
BC = AC - AB = 20 - 8 = 12 m

classifications.value: The postulate is used to set up and solve equations when segment lengths involve variables.
characteristics: Lengths may be given as algebraic expressions (e.g., 2x + 1)
Set up equation: (expression for AB) + (expression for BC) = (expression for AC)
Solve for the variable, then substitute back to find actual lengths
Always check that lengths are positive
classifications.behavior: The Segment Addition Postulate transforms geometric relationships into algebraic equations that can be solved systematically.

examples.explanation:
AB = 2x, BC = 3x + 1, AC = 21. B is between A and C.
Equation: 2x + (3x + 1) = 21
5x + 1 = 21 → 5x = 20 → x = 4
Then AB = 2(4) = 8, BC = 3(4) + 1 = 13
Check: 8 + 13 = 21 ✓

worked_examples.title: Applying the Segment Addition Postulate
worked_examples.problem:
Point B lies between A and C.
1) AB = 6, BC = 11. Find AC.
2) AC = 30, AB = 12. Find BC.
3) AB = 2x - 3, BC = x + 7, AC = 4x. Find x and all three lengths.

step_by_step.explanation:
Since B is between A and C, apply the postulate:
AC = AB + BC
AC = 6 + 11 = 17

Substitute the expressions into AB + BC = AC:
(2x - 3) + (x + 7) = 4x
3x + 4 = 4x

worked_examples.concept_applied: Segment Addition Postulate: If B is between A and C, then AB + BC = AC. Used to find total length, missing parts, and solve algebraic equations.

sections.introduction: When segments have equal length, they are congruent. A midpoint divides a segment into two congruent segments. These concepts combine the Segment Addition Postulate with the idea of equality to solve problems about symmetry and bisection.

key_concept.definition: Congruent segments are segments that have the same length, denoted by the symbol ≅. A midpoint of a segment is the point that divides the segment into two congruent segments—it lies exactly halfway between the endpoints.
key_concept.context: Congruence is a fundamental concept throughout geometry. Midpoints are used to construct perpendicular bisectors, medians of triangles, and to find the center of line segments in coordinate geometry.

classifications.value: Segments with equal length; the geometric figures have the same size.
characteristics: AB— ≅ CD— means the segments are congruent (same length)
If AB— ≅ CD—, then AB = CD (equal numerical lengths)
Congruence is about the figure; equality is about the number
In diagrams, matching tick marks indicate congruence
classifications.behavior: Congruent segments can be in different locations but have identical measurements. Congruence is an equivalence relation (reflexive, symmetric, transitive).

examples.explanation:
If AB = 5 and CD = 5, then AB— ≅ CD—.
The segments have the same length, so they are congruent.

Two segments each marked with a single tick are congruent to each other.
Segments with double ticks are congruent to each other but not necessarily to single-ticked segments.

classifications.value: The point on a segment that divides it into two congruent segments.
characteristics: If M is the midpoint of AB—, then AM— ≅ MB— (so AM = MB)
M lies between A and B, so AM + MB = AB
Since AM = MB, we have AM = MB = AB ÷ 2
Every segment has exactly one midpoint
classifications.behavior: The midpoint is the unique point equidistant from both endpoints. It splits the segment exactly in half.

examples.explanation:
If M is the midpoint of AB— and AB = 14 cm, then
AM = MB = 14 ÷ 2 = 7 cm

M is midpoint of PQ—. PM = 3x + 1, MQ = 5x - 7.
Since M is midpoint, PM = MQ.
3x + 1 = 5x - 7 → 8 = 2x → x = 4
PM = 3(4) + 1 = 13, MQ = 5(4) - 7 = 13
PQ = PM + MQ = 26

worked_examples.title: Midpoint and Congruence Problems
worked_examples.problem:
1) M is the midpoint of XY—. If XM = 9, find MY and XY.
2) B is the midpoint of AC—. AB = 2x + 3, BC = 5x - 9. Find x, AB, BC, and AC.
3) In the diagram, PQ— ≅ QR— and QR— ≅ RS—. If PQ = 4, find PS.

step_by_step.explanation:
Since M is the midpoint of XY—, XM = MY.
MY = 9 (same as XM)
XY = XM + MY = 9 + 9 = 18

B is the midpoint, so AB = BC.
2x + 3 = 5x - 9

PQ— ≅ QR— means PQ = QR = 4
QR— ≅ RS— means QR = RS = 4
Points P, Q, R, S are collinear and in order.
PS = PQ + QR + RS = 4 + 4 + 4 = 12

worked_examples.concept_applied: Midpoint divides a segment into two congruent segments (equal lengths). Congruent segments have equal lengths. Segment Addition Postulate extends to multiple collinear segments.

sections.introduction: A segment bisector is any geometric figure—a point, line, ray, or plane—that passes through the midpoint of a segment. Understanding bisectors connects the concepts of midpoints, congruence, and the Segment Addition Postulate.

key_concept.definition: A segment bisector is a point, line, ray, segment, or plane that intersects a segment at its midpoint, dividing the segment into two congruent segments.
key_concept.context: Bisectors are fundamental in geometric constructions, symmetry, and proofs. The perpendicular bisector of a segment is especially important in later topics involving triangles, circles, and coordinate geometry.

classifications.value: Any geometric figure passing through the midpoint of a segment can bisect it.
characteristics: A point at the midpoint bisects the segment into two congruent segments
A line passing through the midpoint bisects the segment
A ray with endpoint at the midpoint (extending in either direction) bisects the segment
A plane containing the midpoint (in 3D) bisects the segment
classifications.behavior: The bisector must pass through the midpoint. It does not need to be perpendicular—any line through the midpoint is a bisector. A perpendicular bisector is a special case.

examples.explanation:
Line ℓ passes through M, the midpoint of AB—.
Line ℓ is a segment bisector of AB—.
AM— ≅ MB—.

Ray MX has endpoint at M, the midpoint of AB—.
Ray MX bisects AB— because it passes through the midpoint.

classifications.value: A bisector that intersects the segment at a 90° angle at its midpoint.
characteristics: Passes through the midpoint (bisects the segment)
Forms a right angle (90°) with the segment
Every point on the perpendicular bisector is equidistant from the segment's endpoints
Unique to a given segment—there is exactly one perpendicular bisector
classifications.behavior: The perpendicular bisector is the most important type of segment bisector. It combines midpoint and perpendicularity.

examples.explanation:
Line ℓ passes through midpoint M of AB— and is perpendicular to AB—.
Line ℓ is the perpendicular bisector of AB—.
Any point P on ℓ has PA = PB.

image.alt: Left: Segment AB with midpoint M. A line passing through M is labeled 'bisector.' Right: Segment AB with midpoint M. A line passing through M at a right angle is labeled 'perpendicular bisector.' Caption: 'Any line through the midpoint is a bisector; a perpendicular bisector also forms 90°.'
image.caption: Segment Bisectors: Any figure passing through the midpoint bisects the segment. The perpendicular bisector is a special case forming right angles.

sections.section_title: Common Mistakes with Segments and Length
sections.introduction: Several errors commonly occur when working with segment notation, the Segment Addition Postulate, and midpoint problems. Identifying these patterns helps avoid losing points on problems you otherwise understand.

characteristics: Error: Writing 'AB— = 5 cm' (should be 'AB = 5 cm')
Error: Writing 'AB ≅ CD' (should be 'AB— ≅ CD—')
Correct: AB— is the figure; AB is the number; ≅ compares figures; = compares numbers
classifications.behavior: The bar over letters denotes the geometric object (segment). Without the bar, it denotes the length (number). Congruence (≅) is for figures; equality (=) is for numbers.

examples.explanation: Correct: AB— is the segment. AB = 5 cm is its length. AB— ≅ CD— means segments are congruent. AB = CD means lengths are equal.

classifications.note: Solution: Bar = geometric figure. No bar = length (number). Congruence symbol ≅ = figures have same size. Equals sign = numbers have same value.

classifications.value: Applying the Segment Addition Postulate when the point is not between the endpoints.
characteristics: Error: Writing AB + BC = AC when B is not on segment AC
Error: Assuming three points are collinear without being told so
Correct: The postulate only applies if B is between A and C (which implies collinearity)
classifications.behavior: The Segment Addition Postulate requires betweenness, which requires collinearity. If the three points form a triangle, the postulate does not apply—you must use the Triangle Inequality instead.

examples.explanation:
Points A, B, C form a triangle. AB = 3, BC = 4, AC = 5.
It would be wrong to write AB + BC = AC because 3 + 4 ≠ 5, and B is not between A and C on a line. The Triangle Inequality says AB + BC > AC (3 + 4 > 5).

classifications.note: Solution: Verify that the middle point lies on the segment connecting the outer points. Look for the word 'between' or a diagram showing collinearity.

classifications.value: Setting up the wrong equation for midpoint problems.
characteristics: Error: Writing AM + MB = AB and thinking that's enough (it's always true, not a midpoint condition)
Error: Writing AM = AB/2 without checking M is the midpoint
Correct: If M is midpoint, then AM = MB. This is the specific condition to use.
classifications.behavior: The Segment Addition Postulate (AM + MB = AB) is true whenever M is between A and B. The midpoint condition is the additional fact that AM = MB. Both are needed.

examples.explanation:
M is midpoint of AB—.
Equation 1 (always true): AM + MB = AB
Equation 2 (midpoint condition): AM = MB
Together: 2(AM) = AB, so AM = MB = AB/2

classifications.note: Solution: For midpoints, use AM = MB as your starting equation, or use AM = AB/2 if you know the total length. The postulate AM + MB = AB is secondary.

classifications.value: Accepting a negative or zero value as a valid segment length.
characteristics: Error: Getting x = -2 and reporting AB = -4 as the length
Error: Not rejecting solutions that give zero or negative lengths
Correct: Length must be positive. Reject solutions that produce non-positive lengths.
classifications.behavior: Segment length is a distance—it is always a positive number. If solving an equation gives a value of x that makes a length zero or negative, that value must be rejected.

examples.explanation:
AB = 2x - 10, x is found to be 3.
Then AB = 2(3) - 10 = -4.
A length of -4 is impossible. Either x = 3 is wrong, or the expression for AB was set up incorrectly. Re-examine the problem.

classifications.note: Solution: After solving for a variable, always substitute back to find actual lengths. Verify every length is positive. If not, check your equation or reject the solution.

worked_examples.title: Correcting Segment Errors
worked_examples.problem:
A student attempts these problems. Identify and correct each error.
1) 'AB— = 10 cm'
2) Points P, Q, R form a triangle. Student writes: PQ + QR = PR.
3) M is midpoint of XY—. Student writes: XM + MY = XY, so XM = XY/2 without stating XM = MY.

step_by_step.explanation:
Error: AB— is the segment (a figure). It does not equal a number.
Correct: AB = 10 cm (length is a number). Or state: The length of AB— is 10 cm.

Error: The Segment Addition Postulate requires Q to be between P and R on a line. In a triangle, no point is between the other two—they are noncollinear.
Correct: For triangle PQR, the Triangle Inequality states PQ + QR > PR (the sum of two sides is greater than the third).

While the conclusion XM = XY/2 is correct, the reasoning skipped a step. The Segment Addition Postulate gives XM + MY = XY. The midpoint condition gives XM = MY. Substituting: XM + XM = XY → 2(XM) = XY → XM = XY/2. Both equations are needed for a complete argument.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) AB = 10 cm (length, no bar)
2) For a triangle, PQ + QR > PR (Triangle Inequality), not equal
3) XM = MY is the midpoint condition; combine with XM + MY = XY to get XM = XY/2
worked_examples.concept_applied: Notation: bar vs. no bar. Segment Addition Postulate requires collinearity and betweenness. Midpoint requires both the postulate and the equality condition.

sections.introduction: Measuring and calculating segment lengths is one of the most practical skills in geometry, with applications spanning construction, navigation, design, and everyday problem-solving.

categories.description: Builders use segment measurement and the Segment Addition Postulate constantly.

examples.explanation: A carpenter needs to cut a board 12 feet long into two pieces. If the first piece is 5 feet, the second must be 12 - 5 = 7 feet. This is the Segment Addition Postulate in action: total length = part 1 + part 2.
The total fence length is the sum of the individual side segments. If three sides of a rectangular yard measure 40 ft, 60 ft, and 40 ft, the fourth side must be 60 ft to close the rectangle.

categories.description: Distances along routes are calculated using segment addition principles.

examples.explanation: The distance from City A to City C via City B equals the distance from A to B plus the distance from B to C. GPS systems use this principle to calculate total route length by summing segment distances.

A trail is marked with distance posts. If post 1 is at mile 0, post 2 at mile 3.7, and post 3 at mile 8.2, the distance between any two posts is found by subtracting: distance from post 2 to post 3 = 8.2 - 3.7 = 4.5 miles.

categories.description: Interior design and graphic layout involve dividing spaces into proportional segments.

examples.explanation: A wall is 16 feet long. To center a 6-foot sofa, find the midpoint of the wall (8 feet from either end), then place the sofa's midpoint there. The sofa extends 3 feet on each side of the midpoint—segment addition verifies the fit.

A page is 24 picas wide. Dividing it into three equal columns means each column is 8 picas wide. The midpoints of the columns determine where to align text. This is repeated application of dividing a segment into congruent parts.

sections.introduction: Mastery of segment measurement requires practice with notation, the Segment Addition Postulate, midpoints, and algebraic problem-solving. Work through these problems to build confidence.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Segment Practice
worked_examples.problem:
1) B is between A and C. AB = 8, BC = 13. Find AC.
2) B is between A and C. AC = 35, AB = 17. Find BC.
3) B is between A and C. AB = 3x - 2, BC = 2x + 5, AC = 6x - 1. Find x and all lengths.
4) M is the midpoint of PQ—. PM = 4x + 1, MQ = 7x - 8. Find x, PM, MQ, and PQ.
5) In the diagram, AB— ≅ BC— and BC— ≅ CD—. If AD = 36, find AB.

step_by_step.explanation:
AB— ≅ BC— ≅ CD— means AB = BC = CD.
Points A, B, C, D are collinear and in order.
AD = AB + BC + CD = AB + AB + AB = 3(AB)
36 = 3(AB) → AB = 12

worked_examples.concept_applied: Segment Addition Postulate, midpoint condition (AM = MB), congruent segments, algebraic equation solving, and multi-segment chains.

summary: Line segments are finite, measurable portions of lines bounded by two endpoints. Key points: 1) Notation: AB— refers to the segment (the geometric figure); AB refers to its length (a number). Congruent segments (AB— ≅ CD—) have equal length (AB = CD). 2) Segment Addition Postulate: If point B lies between points A and C, then AB + BC = AC. The whole length equals the sum of its parts. This requires collinearity and betweenness. 3) Midpoint: The point M that divides a segment into two congruent segments. If M is the midpoint of AB—, then AM = MB, and AM + MB = AB, so AM = MB = AB ÷ 2. 4) Segment Bisector: Any point, line, ray, or plane that passes through the midpoint of a segment. The perpendicular bisector is a special case that also forms a 90° angle. 5) Algebraic applications: The Segment Addition Postulate and midpoint condition are used to set up equations when lengths are expressed with variables. Always verify that final lengths are positive. 6) Common errors: Confusing segment notation (bar vs. no bar), applying the postulate without collinearity, misidentifying the midpoint condition, and accepting negative lengths. Mastering segments and length measurement is foundational for perimeter, coordinate geometry, and all subsequent geometry topics.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with 'Line Segments' in center. Branches: Notation (AB— = segment, AB = length, ≅ = congruence), Segment Addition Postulate (AB + BC = AC when B is between A and C), Midpoint (AM = MB = AB/2), Bisectors (any figure through midpoint, perpendicular bisector special case). Applications: algebraic equations, construction, navigation.
review_questions_image.caption: Segments and Length Review: A visual concept map connecting notation, the Segment Addition Postulate, midpoints, and bisectors.
